title: Wet felting with plant fibres
subtitle: How to incorporate cotton, linen, and silk into a merino felt base
excerpt: Plant fibres do not felt on their own: cotton, linen, and ramie lack the scales that cause wool to interlock. When layered with merino, however, they can be trapped in the felt matrix to produce interesting texture, colour, and surface effects. This reading explains how plant fibres behave in a merino felt base and how to work with each type.
sourceNotes: The behaviour of plant fibres in a wet-felted merino matrix is well documented in textile guild education. Method draws on observation-led instruction in UK and Australian contemporary felting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fibre whose surface scales allow the wet-felting process to work. Wool and other protein fibres (alpaca, angora, mohair) are feltable because hot water and agitation cause their scales to open and interlock. Plant fibres (cotton, linen, silk) are not feltable by themselves but can be incorporated into a wool felt matrix.

content.text: Merino is the workhorse of the felt base. When you add plant fibres to a merino layout, you are asking the merino to do all the structural work while the plant fibre is physically trapped in the matrix. The difference between a plant fibre that stays and one that falls out is how thoroughly the merino surrounds and locks onto it during the felting process. Understanding
and non-feltable ones helps you predict what each addition will do.

Cotton locks into merino when it is laid in thin, tufted wisps between wool layers. Cotton threads, puffs of raw cotton, or snippets of cotton yarn all work. The cotton adds a white or dyed textural element: it felts in as a contrasting spot or streak against the wool. Cotton does not shrink as much as merino during felting, so thick cotton inclusions create small puckers in the finished surface. This puckering is a feature in some designs (a bumpy, textured cloth) and a problem in others (a flat runner where puckering reads as errors).

Linen adds structural rigidity and a slight sheen. It works best as a pre-woven linen scrim laid under or between wool layers. The linen scrim felts into the matrix rather like silk organza in nuno felting: the wool migrates through the open weave and grips the linen in place. A linen-backed felt panel is stiffer and harder-wearing than a plain merino panel of the same thickness, making it well-suited to bags, book covers, and mats.

Silk locks readily into merino. Silk noil (the short-fibre waste from silk processing) felts in as dense, slightly rough surface inclusions; silk hankies (stretched and thinned silk cocoon layers) felt in as translucent layers with a glassy sheen. Silk threads laid in straight lines across a wet layout produce a striped surface effect. Silk does not shrink as dramatically as wool during fulling, which creates a textured puckering similar to cotton at thick silk inclusions.

Lay the wool base in the normal way. Place the plant fibre inclusions on top of the first wool layer, spread thinly and evenly. Cover with a final wool layer to sandwich the inclusions between wool. Wet out and felt as normal. The sandwiching is critical: plant fibres placed only on the surface without a covering wool layer will not lock in and will pull off easily in the early felting stages.

Plant fibres slow down the felting process slightly: the wool has to work harder to interlock around an inert fibre. Allow 20-30% more felting time than you would for a plain wool panel of the same size. Also sand the felting time toward the longer end of the range: under-felted wool with plant inclusions is weaker than under-felted plain wool because the plant fibre creates weak points if the surrounding wool is not firmly locked.
